title: Latency Budget Calculator Guide
summary: Understand source delay, switching/routing delay, uplink delay, WAN/VPN transport, decode/processing, render time, buffer reserve, and target budget.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool adds the major latency contributors in an end-to-end path and compares the total against a target latency budget.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch network pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it for live video, remote viewing, monitoring, control paths, VPN transport, WAN-connected systems, or any workflow where delay affects usability.
bullets: Early network design planning.
Expansion, migration, or upgrade review.
Existing-system troubleshooting or validation.
Explaining network tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions match the visible inputs on this tool and explain what each value represents in practical network planning terms.

items.label: Source / encode latency
items.description: Delay added before traffic fully enters the network path, usually from source processing, capture, encoding, or device buffering.
items.whyItMatters: Source latency can dominate the total path before the network even contributes much delay.
items.commonMistake: Blaming the network while ignoring encoder or source-device delay.

items.label: Switching / routing
items.description: Delay introduced by forwarding, routing, queueing, inspection, or internal network hops.
items.whyItMatters: Small per-hop delays can add up across switching, routing, and security boundaries.
items.commonMistake: Assuming LAN devices add zero delay under load.

items.label: Uplink / aggregation
items.description: Delay added when traffic crosses uplinks, shared aggregation paths, or congestion-prone boundaries.
items.whyItMatters: Aggregation delay often appears when a shared link is near utilization pressure.
items.commonMistake: Checking average latency without looking at the busy uplink stage.

links.label: Check oversubscription pressure
links.note: Use this when latency is likely caused by shared-link pressure.

items.label: WAN / VPN transport
items.description: Delay introduced by WAN, internet, VPN, tunnel, carrier, or long-haul transport.
items.whyItMatters: WAN and VPN delay can dominate end-to-end responsiveness even when the LAN is healthy.
items.commonMistake: Using LAN expectations for remote or tunneled traffic.

links.label: Account for VPN overhead
links.note: Use this when the latency path includes a tunnel.

items.label: Decode / processing
items.description: Delay added after transport from decode, server-side processing, client-side processing, or application handling.
items.whyItMatters: Processing delay can be as important as network delay for video and interactive workflows.
items.commonMistake: Only measuring transport latency and ignoring application stages.

items.label: Client render
items.description: Final delay from the endpoint, display, browser, video client, or application rendering stage.
items.whyItMatters: A path can meet network targets but still feel slow if the client render stage is high.
items.commonMistake: Stopping latency analysis at the network handoff.

items.label: Jitter buffer / reserve
items.description: Extra delay intentionally added to absorb jitter, smooth playback, or protect against variable transport delay.
items.whyItMatters: Buffers improve stability but increase total delay, so they must be counted in the latency budget.
items.commonMistake: Adding buffer for stability and forgetting it consumes response-time margin.

links.label: Size the jitter buffer directly
links.note: Use this when jitter protection is the main reason for added delay.

items.label: Target latency budget (ms)
items.description: The maximum acceptable end-to-end latency for the use case.
items.whyItMatters: This is the planning boundary that determines whether the total path is acceptable.
items.commonMistake: Setting a target after the design is already built instead of before tradeoffs are made.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means total modeled latency stays below the target with room to spare. Watch means one or more stages deserve review. Risk means the path may feel delayed or fail the intended response-time goal.
bullets: Healthy: the entered assumptions leave practical operating margin.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumption deserves review before relying on it.
Risk: reduce demand, increase capacity, change the topology, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most network planning mistakes come from checking one layer or one average value while ignoring peaks, overhead, path behavior, failure conditions, and growth.
bullets: Planning from theoretical line rate instead of usable sustained throughput.
Sizing for calm-period average traffic while ignoring peaks, bursts, retransmits, and maintenance activity.
Treating one network layer in isolation even though access, aggregation, WAN, VPN, and endpoint behavior interact.
Forgetting to document assumptions before the design becomes hardware, cabling, or service-provider work.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, firewall/router/switch limits, service-provider terms, cabling validation, monitoring data, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Real measurements from the installed network are better than estimates whenever they are available.
Protocol overhead, encapsulation, device behavior, cabling, switching, routing, and WAN conditions can change the result.
Healthy results still need validation against the actual switch, firewall, router, ISP, tunnel, or endpoint platform being used.
A design with documented headroom is usually easier to support, troubleshoot, and grow.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, vendor validation, production testing, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual hardware, firmware, service-provider limits, and network architecture being deployed.
Confirm stakeholder expectations for performance, outage behavior, security, and operational recovery.
Document assumptions before implementation and revisit them as traffic or topology changes.
